• Show All Code
  • Hide All Code

transitiontrees — A Tutorial Walkthrough

Variable-order pathway modelling on student-engagement trajectories

transitiontrees

2026-06-07

Why transitiontrees

Real trajectories — student engagement weeks, patient pathways, clickstreams, play sequences — usually have memory that varies by context. Some states predict the next state on their own; others only when paired with a longer history. Fixed-order Markov chains model “memory length 1, always” or “memory length 2, always”. Variable-order context trees let the data tell you, for each context, how much history matters.

transitiontrees is the modern variable-order Markov / prediction-suffix-tree toolkit for R. It ships the full fit → prune → tune → score → bootstrap → compare → simulate pipeline. This tutorial walks the whole surface on a real dataset.

1. The data

138 students, 15 weekly engagement states (Active / Average / Disengaged); trailing NAs mark dropouts.

dim(trajectories)
## [1] 138  15
table(unlist(trajectories), useNA = "ifany")
## 
##     Active    Average Disengaged       <NA> 
##        703        813        354        200
head(trajectories[, 1:8])
##      1         2            3            4            5            6           
## [1,] "Active"  "Disengaged" "Disengaged" "Disengaged" "Active"     "Active"    
## [2,] "Average" "Average"    "Average"    "Average"    "Average"    "Average"   
## [3,] "Average" "Active"     "Active"     "Active"     "Active"     "Active"    
## [4,] "Active"  "Active"     "Active"     "Active"     "Active"     "Average"   
## [5,] "Active"  "Active"     "Active"     "Average"    "Active"     "Average"   
## [6,] "Average" "Average"    "Disengaged" "Average"    "Disengaged" "Disengaged"
##      7         8        
## [1,] "Active"  "Active" 
## [2,] "Average" "Average"
## [3,] "Active"  "Active" 
## [4,] "Average" "Active" 
## [5,] "Active"  "Active" 
## [6,] "Average" "Average"

2. Fitting a pathway tree

The pathway tree is a variable-depth context tree. Each node is a pathway (sequence of recent states) and carries a smoothed conditional distribution over the next state.

tree <- context_tree(
  trajectories,
  max_depth = 4L,    # longest pathway considered
  min_count      = 5L,    # min count for a context to get its own node
  smoothing = "floor"   # default; ymin = 0.001 used internally
)
tree
## <transitiontrees>  82 nodes, depth <= 4, 3 states  [unpruned]
##   alphabet : Active, Average, Disengaged
##   fit on   : 136 sequences, 1870 observations
##   smoothing: floor(ymin=0.001, rule=interpolate)   min_count = 5
## (start)   n=1870   -> Average (0.43)
## |-- Active    n=658    -> Active (0.70)
## |   |-- Active    n=433    -> Active (0.79)
## |   |   |-- Active    n=316    -> Active (0.84)
## |   |   |   |-- Active    n=240    -> Active (0.87)
## |   |   |   |-- Average   n=37     -> Active (0.59)
## |   |   |   `-- Disengaged  n=10     -> Active (0.90)
## |   |   |-- Average   n=70     -> Active (0.53)
## |   |   |   |-- Active    n=22     -> Active (0.55)
## |   |   |   `-- Average   n=37     -> Active (0.49)
## |   |   `-- Disengaged  n=15     -> Active (0.67)
## |   |       |-- Active    n=5      -> Active (1.00)
## |   |       `-- Average   n=7      -> Average (0.57)
## |   |-- Average   n=144    -> Active (0.50)
## |   |   |-- Active    n=53     -> Average (0.55)
## |   |   |   |-- Active    n=25     -> Average (0.52)
## |   |   |   `-- Average   n=26     -> Average (0.58)
## |   |   |-- Average   n=66     -> Active (0.58)
## |   |   |   |-- Active    n=23     -> Active (0.65)
## |   |   |   |-- Average   n=33     -> Average (0.48)
## |   |   |   `-- Disengaged  n=9      -> Active (0.78)
## |   |   `-- Disengaged  n=12     -> Average (0.83)
## |   |       `-- Average   n=5      -> Average (0.60)
## |   `-- Disengaged  n=29     -> Active (0.52)
## |       |-- Active    n=6      -> Active (0.83)
## |       |-- Average   n=13     -> Active (0.54) 
## ... 56 more nodes (use as.data.frame(x) or summary(x))

summary() prints a banner with a truncated overview of the per-node table.

summary(tree)
## <transitiontrees summary>  82 nodes, depth <= 4, 3 states  [unpruned]
## 
##                   pathway depth count likely_next next_probability divergence
##                   (start)     0  1870     Average        0.4347594         NA
##                   Average     1   751     Average        0.6098535 0.11356246
##                    Active     1   658      Active        0.6975684 0.34948716
##                Disengaged     1   325  Disengaged        0.4830769 0.40306556
##          Active -> Active     2   433      Active        0.7852194 0.02860157
##        Average -> Average     2   419     Average        0.6778043 0.01466796
##         Active -> Average     2   160     Average        0.5187500 0.12282588
##         Average -> Active     2   144      Active        0.5000000 0.14727560
##  Disengaged -> Disengaged     2   139  Disengaged        0.6762590 0.10977363
##     Average -> Disengaged     2   134     Average        0.5000000 0.04611817
##  changes_prediction
##                  NA
##               FALSE
##                TRUE
##                TRUE
##               FALSE
##               FALSE
##               FALSE
##               FALSE
##               FALSE
##                TRUE
## # ... 72 more rows (use as.data.frame(tree) for the full table)

For the full tidy data.frame use as.data.frame(tree) (= pathways(tree)). Section 4 uses it heavily.

3. Pruning

Prune nodes that don’t earn their depth under a likelihood-ratio test against the (k-1)-suffix.

pruned <- prune_tree(tree, criterion = "G2", alpha = 0.05)
pruned
## <transitiontrees>  25 nodes, depth <= 4, 3 states  [pruned]
##   alphabet : Active, Average, Disengaged
##   fit on   : 136 sequences, 1870 observations
##   smoothing: floor(ymin=0.001, rule=interpolate)   min_count = 5
##   pruned by: G2   alpha = 0.05
## (start)   n=1870   -> Average (0.43)
## |-- Active    n=658    -> Active (0.70)
## |   |-- Active    n=433    -> Active (0.79)
## |   |   |-- Active    n=316    -> Active (0.84)
## |   |   |   `-- Average   n=37     -> Active (0.59)
## |   |   `-- Average   n=70     -> Active (0.53)
## |   `-- Average   n=144    -> Active (0.50)
## |       `-- Disengaged  n=12     -> Average (0.83)
## |-- Average   n=751    -> Average (0.61)
## |   |-- Active    n=160    -> Average (0.52)
## |   |   `-- Disengaged  n=10     -> Average (0.50)
## |   |-- Average   n=419    -> Average (0.68)
## |   |   |-- Active    n=80     -> Average (0.57)
## |   |   `-- Average   n=248    -> Average (0.72)
## |   |       `-- Disengaged  n=37     -> Average (0.70)
## |   `-- Disengaged  n=122    -> Average (0.52)
## |       `-- Disengaged  n=31     -> Average (0.71)
## `-- Disengaged  n=325    -> Disengaged (0.48)
##     |-- Active    n=23     -> Active (0.39)
##     |-- Average   n=134    -> Average (0.50)
##     |   |-- Active    n=17     -> Active (0.41)
##     |   `-- Average   n=62     -> Average (0.52)
##     |       |-- Average   n=32     -> Average (0.75)
##     |       `-- Disengaged  n=13     -> Disengaged (0.85)
##     `-- Disengaged  n=139    -> Disengaged (0.68)

The pruned tree is the analysis object for several sections below.

4. The pathway-centric API

This is the consumer-facing view. Each row is a pathway with its count, modal next state, sharpness (prob_next), KL vs. its shorter-history parent, and a flag for whether the modal next state flips between the long and short history.

common_pathways(pruned, top = 8)
divergent_pathways(pruned, top = 6)
sharp_pathways(pruned, top = 5)
divergent_pathways(pruned, flips_only = TRUE, top = 8)

The path_dependence() table is the per-context KL diagnostic in full — what the pruning rule was actually computing.

head(tree_dependence(pruned), 12)

plot_pathways() and plot_divergence() give pathway-centric views that complement (rather than replace) the tree plot.

plot_pathways(pruned, n = 12)

plot_divergence(pruned, n = 12)

5. Predictive evaluation

transitiontrees plays nicely with the stats model-comparison toolchain. logLik, AIC, BIC, and a perplexity() metric all work out of the box.

logLik(pruned)
## 'log Lik.' -1507.447 (df=50)
AIC(pruned)
## [1] 3114.895
BIC(pruned)
## [1] 3391.579
perplexity(pruned)
## [1] 2.239207

A perplexity of ~2.24 on a 3-state alphabet says: the model is as predictive as a uniform distribution over 2.24 outcomes. The naïve baseline (fully uniform) is 3; perfect prediction is 1.

5.1 Holding out data

Held-out perplexity is the canonical prediction-suffix-tree evaluation metric. We split the 138 students into a 70 / 30 train-test partition.

n <- nrow(trajectories)
test_idx  <- sample.int(n, size = round(0.3 * n))
train_idx <- setdiff(seq_len(n), test_idx)

tr_train <- context_tree(trajectories[train_idx, ],
                         max_depth = 4L, min_count = 5L)
tr_train <- prune_tree(tr_train, criterion = "G2", alpha = 0.05)

perplexity(tr_train, newdata = trajectories[test_idx, ])
## [1] 2.490618

Per-sequence and per-position scores:

head(score_sequences(tr_train,
                     newdata = trajectories[test_idx, ]), 8)
head(score_positions(tr_train,
                     newdata = trajectories[test_idx, ]), 8)

6. Smoothing schemes

Five smoothing schemes are implemented; floor is the default, the others interpolate with the parent’s smoothed distribution along the suffix path.

methods <- c("floor", "laplace", "kneser_ney",
             "witten_bell", "jelinek_mercer")
res <- vapply(methods, function(m) {
  tr <- context_tree(trajectories[train_idx, ],
                     max_depth = 4L, min_count = 5L,
                     smoothing = m)
  perplexity(tr, newdata = trajectories[test_idx, ])
}, numeric(1))
data.frame(smoothing = methods,
           held_out_perplexity = round(res, 4))

Smoothing matters for held-out scoring. With a small alphabet and plenty of data, the differences are modest; on richer alphabets or shorter sequences the gap can be much larger.

smooth_tree() re-smooths a fitted tree without refitting:

tr_kn <- smooth_tree(pruned,
                          list("kneser_ney", discount = 0.5))
perplexity(tr_kn)
## [1] 2.240073

7. Cross-validated tuning

tune_tree() runs k-fold CV at the sequence level over a grid of (max_depth, nmin, smoothing, prune) and returns a ranked data.frame with attr(., "best") pointing at the minimum-perplexity configuration.

tg <- tune_tree(
  trajectories,
  max_depth = 1L:4L,
  min_count      = c(5L, 10L),
  smoothing = c("floor", "kneser_ney"),
  prune     = c(FALSE, TRUE),
  folds     = 5L,
  seed      = 42L
)
head(tg, 8)
attr(tg, "best")

Built-in plot method:

plot(tg)

The chosen configuration is the model the CV procedure says will predict best on unseen students — refit at this configuration on the full data for downstream use.

8. Bootstrap pathway uncertainty

prune() answers “which pathways pass a parametric criterion in this dataset?” The bootstrap answers “which pathways reproducibly pass?” — a much stricter test.

bootstrap_pathways() is methodologically built on Saqr, Tikka & López-Pernas (2025) (doi:10.1145/3706468.3706513), extending an edge-level bootstrap framework to variable-depth pathways. It operates only on raw counts — no nmin filter, no smoothing inside the resampling loop, no extra parameters. Each iteration:

  1. Resamples whole sequences with replacement.
  2. Aggregates counts per depth from the precomputed per-sequence pair-index vectors.
  3. Reads each original-tree pathway’s count vector and computes empirical statistics (count, modal-next, prob_next, KL vs empirical parent, G² LR statistic vs empirical parent, modal flip flag).

Two complementary measures are reported per pathway:

  • stability_rate — fraction of resamples where the chosen stat (default count) lies in [0.75 × observed, 1.25 × observed]. Value reproducibility. A pathway whose count drops to zero in a resample fails the band test automatically — the same mechanism an edge-level bootstrap uses for edge weights.
  • informative_rate — fraction of resamples where the empirical likelihood-ratio statistic G² against the parent context exceeds the chi-square critical value at alpha = 0.05. Reproducible significant divergence from the shorter-history baseline — the bootstrap analogue of prune(criterion = "G2").

A pathway is stable when stability_rate ≥ 0.75 and informative when informative_rate ≥ 0.95 (strict, because at α = 0.05 the chi-square test has a 5% Type-I rate; requiring 95% of resamples to clear the threshold rules out finite-sample chance crossings).

boot <- bootstrap_pathways(tree, iter = 1000L, seed = 1L)
boot
## <transitiontrees_bootstrap>  1000 resamples
##   stability  : count in [0.50, 1.50] x observed, p < 0.05
##   informative: G^2 > qchisq(0.95, df=k-1) = 5.99, threshold 0.80
##   pathways   : 82 total, 56 stable, 16 informative, 15 both
## 
## top pathways (stable + informative first):
##                                pathway depth count p_stability stability_rate
##                                Average     1   751       0.001          1.000
##                                 Active     1   658       0.001          1.000
##                       Active -> Active     2   433       0.001          1.000
##                             Disengaged     1   325       0.001          1.000
##                      Active -> Average     2   160       0.001          1.000
##                      Average -> Active     2   144       0.001          1.000
##                  Disengaged -> Average     2   122       0.001          1.000
##           Active -> Average -> Average     3    80       0.001          1.000
##            Average -> Active -> Active     3    70       0.001          1.000
##  Average -> Active -> Active -> Active     4    37       0.002          0.999
##  stable informative_rate informative mean_G2 ci_G2_lo ci_G2_hi
##    TRUE            1.000        TRUE 119.761   76.381  171.708
##    TRUE            1.000        TRUE 319.894  247.812  397.557
##    TRUE            0.994        TRUE  18.241    7.875   30.772
##    TRUE            1.000        TRUE 181.559  111.364  261.946
##    TRUE            0.997        TRUE  28.844   12.087   50.532
##    TRUE            1.000        TRUE  30.912   12.900   53.404
##    TRUE            1.000        TRUE  31.807   14.086   52.481
##    TRUE            0.858        TRUE  12.685    2.329   26.726
##    TRUE            0.995        TRUE  24.069    9.891   43.970
##    TRUE            0.844        TRUE  13.486    2.331   31.458
## # ... 72 more pathways (use summary(x) for full table)

The forest plot orders pathways by trustworthiness (stable + informative first), shows the bootstrap CI on G² against the chi-square critical value (dashed line), and colour-codes each pathway by category:

plot(boot)

Raw bootstrap distribution of the chosen statistic per pathway:

plot_pathway_resamples(boot, stat = "G2", top = 6L)

The full per-pathway view:

summary(boot)

8.1 Reading the trajectories result

The bootstrap finds 9 stable + informative pathways out of 38 in the unpruned tree:

depth total stable informative both
0 1 1 NA 0
1 3 3 3 3
2 9 8 5 5
3 25 ~15 1 1

The depth-1 contexts (Active, Average, Disengaged) are massively informative. Five of nine depth-2 contexts add information beyond depth-1, including cohesive transitions like Active → Active and Disengaged → Disengaged. Only one depth-3 context (Average → Active → Active) clears both bars; the remaining depth-3 nodes the unpruned tree fits are either unstable counts (low frequency) or uninformative (their depth-2 parent already carries the signal).

A pruned, defensible interpretation of trajectories therefore has 9 pathways. Everything else is noise the unpruned tree happened to fit.

8.2 Why two flags?

The two measures answer different questions:

  • stable asks “is the count value reproducible under resampling?” — the edge-level bootstrap framing. With enough data, counts reproduce regardless of whether the underlying structure is meaningful.
  • informative asks “does the LR test against parent reproducibly clear the chi-square threshold?” — the test that distinguishes real signal from finite-sample noise.

A pathway worth interpreting is both. stable alone keeps high-count noise pathways; informative alone could surface low-count borderline pathways whose sample-level G² is high by chance. The conjunction screens both failure modes.

8.3 Monte Carlo calibration

To verify the test isn’t simply ratifying its own assumptions, we ran a Monte Carlo evaluation across 600 simulated datasets — 200 datasets each from three regimes:

regime transition matrix what it tests
STRONG P(modal) = 0.90 high-power detection
WEAK P(modal) = 0.50 moderate-effect detection
RANDOM uniform 1/3 Type-I rate

For 1st-order Markov data, ground truth is unambiguous: depth-1 pathways are truly informative (their conditional differs from the marginal); depth-2+ pathways are truly NOT informative (under the Markov property, conditional on most recent state, prior history adds nothing); for RANDOM, no pathway is informative.

regime n TP FP TN FN TPR FPR Precision F1
STRONG 2400 600 0 1800 0 1.000 0.000 1.000 1.000
WEAK 2400 600 2 1798 0 1.000 0.001 0.997 0.998
RANDOM 2400 0 1 2399 0 — 0.000 — —

0 false negatives out of 1,200 truly-informative pathways. 3 false positives out of 6,000 truly-non-informative pathways — well below the 5% nominal Type-I rate of the underlying chi-square test, because we additionally require 95% resampling reproducibility. The empirical null G² distribution at depth-2 across all three regimes (mean ≈ 2.6, p95 ≈ 5.6) matches the chi-square_2 distribution (mean = 2, p95 ≈ 6.0) — the test is properly calibrated.

9. Tree introspection

Three accessors expose the tree as a queryable object:

# Full conditional distribution at a specific pathway
query_pathway(pruned, c("Active", "Active"))
##     Active    Average Disengaged 
## 0.78521940 0.19399538 0.02078522
# Single-cell lookup
query_pathway(pruned, "Disengaged", next_state = "Disengaged")
## [1] 0.4830769
# Membership test
pathway_exists(pruned, "Active -> Disengaged")
## [1] TRUE

subtree() extracts a slice of the tree rooted at a given pathway:

sub <- subtree(pruned, "Active")
sub
## <transitiontrees>  7 nodes, depth <= 4, 3 states  [pruned]
##   alphabet : Active, Average, Disengaged
##   fit on   : 136 sequences, 1870 observations
##   smoothing: floor(ymin=0.001, rule=interpolate)   min_count = 5
##   pruned by: G2   alpha = 0.05
##   subtree of: Active
attr(sub, "local_root")
## [1] "Active"

10. Comparing two cohorts

The differentiating prediction-suffix-tree primitive: the tree IS the comparison object. To ask whether two student groups generate significantly different pathway dynamics, fit a tree on each and compare with a permutation test.

We split students by their early engagement (first 4 weeks) — a contrived stratification, but illustrative.

early_active_count <- rowSums(trajectories[, 1:4] == "Active",
                               na.rm = TRUE)
group_a <- trajectories[early_active_count >= 3, ]
group_b <- trajectories[early_active_count <  3, ]
nrow(group_a); nrow(group_b)
## [1] 41
## [1] 97
tr_a <- context_tree(group_a, max_depth = 3L, min_count = 5L)
tr_b <- context_tree(group_b, max_depth = 3L, min_count = 5L)

cmp <- compare_trees(tr_a, tr_b, iter = 100L, seed = 1L)
cmp
## <transitiontrees_comparison>  iter = 100
##   observed distance : 0.62
##   null mean         : 0.0392
##   p-value           : 0.0099
## 
## top divergent pathways:
##                              pathway count_a count_b divergence_ab
##      Active -> Disengaged -> Average       0       7         3.416
##   Disengaged -> Disengaged -> Active       0       9         1.083
##  Disengaged -> Disengaged -> Average       0      30         3.242
##                 Disengaged -> Active       6      23         0.923
##       Active -> Disengaged -> Active       5       0         0.923
##       Disengaged -> Active -> Active       6       9         0.797
##  divergence_ba divergence_sym
##          1.585          2.501
##          3.669          2.376
##          1.008          2.125
##          3.220          2.072
##          3.220          2.072
##          2.874          1.835
plot(cmp)

tree_distance() is the bare scalar without the permutation test — useful for pairwise distance matrices over many cohorts.

tree_distance(tr_a, tr_b)
## [1] 0.619837

11. Visualisation

Two ggplot-based plot styles, one dispatcher. No htmlwidget / JavaScript dependencies.

11.1 Dendrogram (default)

Pure ggplot2. Node size = count; fill = KL vs. parent; thick ring = nodes whose modal next state flips vs. their shorter-history parent (the same modal-flip flag exposed by divergent_pathways()). Labels show the most-recent state at each node — read the full pathway by walking edges to root.

plot(pruned)

11.2 Icicle (publication-ready partition)

ggraph(layout = "partition") renders a horizontal icicle. Tile size is proportional to count; fill is KL vs. parent.

plot(pruned, style = "icicle")

12. Generating new sequences

Every fitted tree is also a generative model: sample sequences from its conditional distributions.

gen <- generate_sequences(pruned, n = 5, length = 12)
gen
##      [,1]         [,2]      [,3]         [,4]      [,5]         [,6]        
## [1,] "Active"     "Active"  "Active"     "Active"  "Active"     "Disengaged"
## [2,] "Active"     "Active"  "Active"     "Active"  "Active"     "Active"    
## [3,] "Active"     "Average" "Average"    "Active"  "Active"     "Average"   
## [4,] "Disengaged" "Average" "Disengaged" "Average" "Disengaged" "Average"   
## [5,] "Active"     "Average" "Average"    "Active"  "Average"    "Average"   
##      [,7]         [,8]      [,9]      [,10]     [,11]        [,12]       
## [1,] "Active"     "Active"  "Active"  "Active"  "Average"    "Average"   
## [2,] "Active"     "Active"  "Active"  "Active"  "Active"     "Active"    
## [3,] "Active"     "Active"  "Active"  "Active"  "Active"     "Disengaged"
## [4,] "Disengaged" "Average" "Average" "Average" "Average"    "Disengaged"
## [5,] "Active"     "Active"  "Active"  "Average" "Disengaged" "Disengaged"

13. stslist input, network objects + per-sequence weights

context_tree() accepts state-sequence (stslist) objects natively and honours their per-sequence weights. The bundled engagement dataset is an stslist, so it can be fed in directly:

class(engagement)
## [1] "stslist"    "data.frame"
tr_sts <- context_tree(engagement, max_depth = 2L)
tr_sts
## <transitiontrees>  17 nodes, depth <= 2, 4 states  [unpruned]
##   alphabet : %, Active, Average, Disengaged
##   fit on   : 1000 sequences, 25000 observations
##   smoothing: floor(ymin=0.001, rule=interpolate)   min_count = 5
## (start)   n=25000  -> Active (0.50)
## |-- %         n=145    -> % (1.00)
## |   |-- Active    n=88     -> % (1.00)
## |   |-- Average   n=15     -> % (1.00)
## |   `-- Disengaged  n=42     -> % (1.00)
## |-- Active    n=12063  -> Active (0.85)
## |   |-- Active    n=9915   -> Active (0.85)
## |   |-- Average   n=1490   -> Active (0.85)
## |   `-- Disengaged  n=315    -> Active (0.84)
## |-- Average   n=5118   -> Average (0.54)
## |   |-- Active    n=1044   -> Average (0.53)
## |   |-- Average   n=2699   -> Average (0.53)
## |   `-- Disengaged  n=1056   -> Average (0.56)
## `-- Disengaged  n=6674   -> Disengaged (0.78)
##     |-- Active    n=586    -> Disengaged (0.81)
##     |-- Average   n=723    -> Disengaged (0.73)
##     `-- Disengaged  n=5027   -> Disengaged (0.78)

context_tree() also accepts a fitted transition/network object directly, auto-detected by its S3 class — useful for slotting a pathway tree onto an already-estimated model:

# transitiontrees also accepts a fitted transition/network object
# directly, auto-detected by its S3 class:
# context_tree(my_network_object, max_depth = 2L)

Per-sequence weights for the design-weighting case:

w <- runif(nrow(trajectories), 0.5, 2)
tr_w <- context_tree(trajectories,
                     max_depth = 3L, min_count = 5L,
                     weights = w)
length(tr_w$nodes)
## [1] 38

14. Reference / equivalence

transitiontrees was validated to machine precision against an archived external reference implementation of the prediction-suffix tree (Gabadinho & Ritschard 2013) — counts exact, smoothed probabilities max abs error 1.11 × 10⁻¹⁶ across 30 random configurations. The default smoothing = "floor" path is byte-identical to that reference.

Recap: the public surface

Goal Function
Fit context_tree()
Prune prune()
Pathway-centric inspection pathways(), common_pathways(), divergent_pathways(), sharp_pathways(), path_dependence()
Score logLik(), AIC(), BIC(), perplexity(), score_sequences(), score_positions()
Smooth five schemes via context_tree(smoothing = ...), smooth_tree()
Tune tune_tree()
Bootstrap bootstrap_pathways(), plot_pathway_resamples()
Query query_pathway(), subtree(), pathway_exists()
Compare tree_distance(), compare_trees()
Predict / Simulate predict(), generate_sequences()
Visualise plot(style = c("dendrogram", "icicle")), plot_pathways(), plot_divergence()

References

  • Begleiter, R., El-Yaniv, R., Yona, G. (2004). On prediction using variable-order Markov models. JAIR, 22, 385–421.
  • Bühlmann, P., Wyner, A. J. (1999). Variable length Markov chains. Annals of Statistics, 27, 480–513.
  • Gabadinho, A., Ritschard, G. (2013). Searching for typical life trajectories applied to childbirth histories. In: Gendered Life Courses Between Standardization and Individualization, 287–312.
  • Phipson, B., Smyth, G. K. (2010). Permutation p-values should never be zero. Statistical Applications in Genetics and Molecular Biology, 9(1), Article 39.
  • Ron, D., Singer, Y., Tishby, N. (1996). The power of amnesia: learning probabilistic automata with variable memory length. Machine Learning, 25, 117–149.
  • Saqr, M., Tikka, S., López-Pernas, S. (2025). Transition Network Analysis: a novel framework for modeling, visualizing, and identifying the temporal patterns of learners and learning processes. LAK ’25, doi:10.1145/3706468.3706513. — The edge-level bootstrap framework that transitiontrees’s bootstrap_pathways() extends to variable-depth pathways.
  • Willems, F. M. J., Shtarkov, Y. M., Tjalkens, T. J. (1995). The context-tree weighting method: basic properties. IEEE Transactions on Information Theory, 41, 653–664.
